checks.a_analysis: The visual organization of the text content faithfully represents the source document's hierarchy. The conversion from a multi-column slide deck layout to a single-column scrolling HTML page is a standard and acceptable practice for web responsiveness. While the document's visual impact is reduced due to missing images (addressed in V3), the structural skeleton—headers and lists—is correctly preserved in a logical flow.

The source PDF does not contain any native tables. The tables visible on pages 16 and 18 of the PDF are screenshots of a website used for illustrative purposes, rather than structured data tables within the document itself. Therefore, there are no tables to evaluate for rendering defects.

The vast majority of meaningful images from the source document are missing from the HTML output. This includes critical evidence of the website redesign such as 'before and after' screenshots (pages 12, 15, 18, 21, 23, 26), accessibility tool screenshots (pages 13, 14, 16, 17), and conceptual diagrams (pages 8, 9, 24). Since the entire document is about a visual redesign and accessibility improvements, these images are highly meaningful and their absence significantly degrades the document's utility.
checks.b_evidence_source: Side-by-side screenshots of old and new website (page 12); 'Evolution of the Mobile Phone' image (page 8); accessibility button contrast examples (page 14); '44 rule' touch target diagrams (page 24).
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML output shows only the title headings for these sections followed by text lists or empty space, with no corresponding elements for the aforementioned screenshots.
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections: 'New Web - Mobile First and Mobile Device Use', 'Changes to SOS Website Before and After', 'Accessibility - Button Contrast', 'Accessibility Challenges Old Website', 'Improved Presentation on New Site', '44 rule'

checks.a_analysis: Several section headings are visually duplicated in the main flow of the HTML document. Each affected heading appears twice consecutively—once as a primary header and once as a sub-header—without any content between them. This occurs for sections like 'Follow Federal Template' and 'Changes to SOS Website Before and After'.
checks.b_evidence_source: Follow Federal Template (page 5); Comply with ADA Title II Accessibility Requirements (page 7); New Web - Mobile First and Mobile Device Use (page 8); Changes to SOS Website Before and After (page 11).
checks.b_evidence_output: The headings 'Follow Federal Template', 'Comply with ADA Title II Accessibility Requirements', 'New Web - Mobile First and Mobile Device Use', and 'Changes to SOS Website Before and After' each appear twice in immediate succession.
checks.b_evidence_location: Main body content after 'Modernize KB Technology' and 'Comply with ADA Title II Accessibility Requirements' sections.
